upcomingEvents.title: Upcoming Events
upcomingEvents.body: The current event stack shows 10 demand windows inside 14 days and 11 inside 30 days, which means 47.6% of the visible 21-event inventory is front-loaded into the next two weeks. Kevin should treat the EDC window as the primary outreach clock.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Map near-term event concentration to procurement and service-demand timing so account outreach aligns with real demand spikes.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: When nearly half of the tracked event inventory is compressed into the next 14 days, sales and service timing become a load-management problem instead of a generic awareness problem.
quantResearchProtocol: Count events by 14-day and 30-day windows and compute the front-load ratio.
Identify whether the next two weeks carry a disproportionate share of total visible demand triggers.
Use concentration math to set outreach urgency.
Translate event compression into service-readiness sequencing.
inferenceConstraints: Do not treat the 30-day calendar as evenly distributed when the near-term load is compressed.
Do not separate event insight from outreach timing.
Do not issue urgency without showing the concentration math.
outputRequirements: State the event counts by window and the front-load ratio.
Explain why the near-term window matters operationally.
Give a concrete outreach timing recommendation.

sourceRecords.recordHint: EDC Week and Electric Daisy Carnival event dates used for 14d/30d counts

notes: Upcoming-events math is based on the public EDC calendar plus the current future-event set.
Operational focus is the next-two-week outreach window, not generic event awareness.

aiSalesStrategy.title: AI Sales Strategy
aiSalesStrategy.body: Ten accounts already clear the active priority threshold, and the next two-week EDC cluster argues for calling high-score accounts before broad prospecting. The sequencing logic remains score threshold first, event urgency second, and service readiness third while CL sits at 95.42 and energy remains the lead buyer risk channel.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Generate account-targeted sales strategy that links oil-cost control narrative to each customer’s demand timing and service profile.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Sales efficiency rises when Kevin leads with the accounts already showing both priority-score support and imminent event-driven demand pressure.
quantResearchProtocol: Count accounts above the active priority threshold and compare that count with near-term event load.
Sequence account outreach by score support first and event urgency second.
Translate the ranking into a field-usable cadence rather than generic messaging.
Retain service readiness as a gating constraint on sales promises.
inferenceConstraints: Do not dilute focus by spreading outreach evenly across all accounts.
Do not recommend aggressive selling detached from service readiness.
Do not hide the threshold logic behind generic AI wording.
outputRequirements: State the threshold and qualified-account count.
Provide the operational reason that supports the sequence.

sourceRecords.recordHint: 10 accounts above current priority threshold in AI snapshot layer
energy-led buyer backdrop used for field messaging posture

notes: Sales sequencing is threshold-driven and event-aware.
Oil-cost backdrop is used to tighten urgency language, not to replace account prioritization.

restaurantAccounts.title: Restaurant Accounts
restaurantAccounts.body: There are 10 active tracked accounts in the current AI layer, and all 10 are still effectively high-priority because the score-threshold set and the active-account set overlap completely in this snapshot. That makes sequencing discipline more important than additional account discovery.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Identify highest-value account opportunities with explicit reasoning tied to event sensitivity, volume pattern, and service window alignment.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: When the high-priority count equals the active tracked-account count, the decision problem shifts from account selection to sequencing and service coordination.
quantResearchProtocol: Compare active-account count with the high-priority count to measure opportunity concentration.
Determine whether the list is selective or saturated.
If saturated, prioritize by timing and service readiness rather than by broad account elimination.
Express the result in field-operations language.
inferenceConstraints: Do not pretend the list is selective when the high-priority count equals the active count.
Do not use one-dimensional priority talk once the opportunity set is saturated.
Do not omit the need for sequencing discipline.
outputRequirements: State the active count, high-priority count, and what that implies.
Explain whether the opportunity set is concentrated or saturated.

sourceRecords.recordHint: 10 active tracked-account rows used in AI snapshot layer
high-priority count equals active-account count in current snapshot
event compression used to turn overlap into sequencing guidance

notes: Opportunity set is saturated in this snapshot.
Operational focus is sequencing and service coordination, not account filtering.

fryerTracking.title: Fryer Equipment Tracking
fryerTracking.body: Fryer rows exist for every tracked account, which is a coverage positive, but equipment-count telemetry is still pending direct sync. Operationally that means service planning can be complete on identity coverage, but not yet on equipment-intensity precision during the EDC demand window.

strategicSpecialInstructions.strategicObjective: Assess fryer lifecycle risk against demand windows to prevent service disruption and emergency replacement cost exposure.
strategicSpecialInstructions.neuralConnectionThesis: Full row coverage without full equipment telemetry creates a false sense of completeness unless service teams explicitly separate known-account coverage from known-load precision.
quantResearchProtocol: Confirm whether every active account has a fryer row.
Separate coverage completeness from telemetry completeness.
Use upcoming event load to decide whether telemetry gaps are operationally tolerable.
Translate the gap into a service-risk statement for field teams.
inferenceConstraints: Do not call the fryer dataset complete when telemetry detail is still pending.
Do not ignore event timing when evaluating equipment data gaps.
Do not provide service guidance without stating the precision limit.
outputRequirements: State row coverage status and telemetry limitation separately.
Recommend the operational posture until telemetry is complete.

sourceRecords.recordHint: coverage rows exist across all tracked accounts, but fryer_count telemetry remains pending
active account roster used for coverage check
EDC-driven urgency used to state the operational precision limit

notes: Coverage completeness and telemetry completeness remain separate states.
The card preserves the operational limitation instead of masking it with generic completeness wording.
